As for lodging, records indicate that a hot spring inn was established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), but it soon closed after becoming a gambling den for laborers working at Sulfur Mountain. Later, in [[:en:1904|1904]] (Meiji 37), a hot spring inn built in Russian style was established, which is considered the origin of the current Kawayu Onsen.
Until the Taishō period, only that one inn operated modestly, but during the Shōwa period, with the opening of automobile roads, the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]] opened in [[:en:1930|1930]] (Shōwa 5), and in [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) the {{ill|Akan National Park|ja|阿寒国立公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan National Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿寒摩周国立公園|1=Akan Mashu National Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415672}} was established, which spurred a rapid increase in therapeutic bathers.<ref>[http://www.iionsen.com/rekishi/donna.html What is Kawayu Onsen?] {{ill|Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja|川湯観光ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref>
During the 【[Second World War]】, tourism demand cooled, and soon after the war in [[:en:1948|1948]] (Shōwa 23), a major fire struck the area,<ref>[http://www.town.teshikaga.hokkaido.jp/teshikaga/gaiyou/gaiyou_data.html Timeline of the Town] [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]</ref> among other hardships. However, following the massive success of the film "{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Your Name|1=Kimi no Na wa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416227}}"—which featured the area around [[:en:Lake Kussharo|Kussharo Lake]] as a filming location—tourism surged once again, and the hot spring town rapidly developed.
Until the 1970s, winter road closures were common, but subsequent improvements in snow removal and road maintenance have established a system that attracts hot spring guests year-round. In its peak year, [[:en:1991|1991]] (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper=[[:en:Hokkaido Shimbun|Hokkaido Shimbun]]|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the [[:en:Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|Great East Japan Earthquake]] in [[:en:2011|2011]] (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the [[:en:2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake]] in [[:en:2018|2018]] (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of [[:en:2020|2020]] (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]]. * By Private Car: Via the [[:en:Dōtō Expressway|Dōtō Expressway]] from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along [[:en:Japan National Route 241|National Route 241]] and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]]. == References ==

'''Kawayu Onsen''' (川湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]. Its name is an Ainu translation of “セセキ（hot）ペツ（river）.” A high-temperature hot spring river flows through the town.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}} {{ill|Acidic springs|ja|酸性泉|lt=Acidic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acidic springs|qq=}} {{ill|Alum springs|ja|含アルミニウム泉|lt=Alum spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alum springs|qq=}} * The water stings the eyes and wounds, and can even dissolve nails; because it corrodes precious metals, caution (such as not wearing wristwatches) is advised. * {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} precipitates and settles in the bath.
== Hot Spring Town == Centered on the source (湯元) of the hot spring river, the town is formed by over 20 establishments including [[:en:Hotel|hotels]], [[:en:Ryokan|ryokan]], souvenir shops, and restaurants. A hot spring river flows through the town, filling the area with steam and the scent of sulfur,<ref>[http://210.196.131.212/infobirds/spa/kensaku/onsenchi_detail.cfm?ID=207]{{ill|Japan Hot Spring Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Hot Spring Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60987269}}</ref> and there are also free foot baths as well as paid {{ill|communal baths|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/communal baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}}. Although the water quantity is abundant, its strongly acidic nature makes it unsuitable for recirculating equipment; therefore, all facilities use a "Flowing Bath" (flow-through) system, a point some establishments proudly promote with a "Source Flowing Bath Declaration."
In the area surrounding the hot spring town, there are also the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]]’s Kawayu Eco-Museum Center, the {{ill|Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|ja|大鵬相撲記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|1=Taihō Sumo Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487390}} (commemorating the achievements of the 48th Yokozuna [[:en:Taihō Kōki|Taihō Kōki]], who spent his childhood here), an indoor pool utilizing the hot spring heat, and the Kawayu Shrine, which gathers local devotional faith. === Kawayu Ekimae Onsen === A free foot bath is located in front of [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]]. Between the station and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]], there are two hot spring hotels/ryokan. The water quality here differs from that of Kawayu Onsen proper, being neutral and typically of the {{ill|sodium bicarbonate hot spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sodium bicarbonate hot spring|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} type. Some guides and introduction sites occasionally lump this facility together with Kawayu Onsen.
== History == Although the hot spring river has long been known to flow through the area, the origin of Kawayu as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} is uncertain. As for lodging, records indicate that a hot spring inn was established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), but it soon closed after becoming a gambling den for laborers working at Sulfur Mountain. Later, in [[:en:1904|1904]] (Meiji 37), a hot spring inn built in Russian style was established, which is considered the origin of the current Kawayu Onsen.
Until the Taishō period, only that one inn operated modestly, but during the Shōwa period, with the opening of automobile roads, the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]] opened in [[:en:1930|1930]] (Shōwa 5), and in [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) the {{ill|Akan National Park|ja|阿寒国立公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan National Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿寒摩周国立公園|1=Akan Mashu National Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415672}} was established, which spurred a rapid increase in therapeutic bathers.<ref>[http://www.iionsen.com/rekishi/donna.html What is Kawayu Onsen?] {{ill|Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja|川湯観光ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref>
During the 【[Second World War]】, tourism demand cooled, and soon after the war in [[:en:1948|1948]] (Shōwa 23), a major fire struck the area,<ref>[http://www.town.teshikaga.hokkaido.jp/teshikaga/gaiyou/gaiyou_data.html Timeline of the Town] [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]</ref> among other hardships. However, following the massive success of the film "{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Your Name|1=Kimi no Na wa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416227}}"—which featured the area around [[:en:Lake Kussharo|Kussharo Lake]] as a filming location—tourism surged once again, and the hot spring town rapidly developed.
Until the 1970s, winter road closures were common, but subsequent improvements in snow removal and road maintenance have established a system that attracts hot spring guests year-round. In its peak year, [[:en:1991|1991]] (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper=[[:en:Hokkaido Shimbun|Hokkaido Shimbun]]|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the [[:en:Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|Great East Japan Earthquake]] in [[:en:2011|2011]] (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the [[:en:2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake]] in [[:en:2018|2018]] (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of [[:en:2020|2020]] (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]]. * By Private Car: Via the [[:en:Dōtō Expressway|Dōtō Expressway]] from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along [[:en:Japan National Route 241|National Route 241]] and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]]. == References ==

== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}} {{ill|Acidic springs|ja|酸性泉|lt=Acidic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acidic springs|qq=}} {{ill|Alum springs|ja|含アルミニウム泉|lt=Alum spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alum springs|qq=}} * The water stings the eyes and wounds, and can even dissolve nails; because it corrodes precious metals, caution (such as not wearing wristwatches) is advised. * {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} precipitates and settles in the bath.
== Hot Spring Town == Centered on the source (湯元) of the hot spring river, the town is formed by over 20 establishments including [[:en:Hotel|hotels]], [[:en:Ryokan|ryokan]], souvenir shops, and restaurants. A hot spring river flows through the town, filling the area with steam and the scent of sulfur,<ref>[http://210.196.131.212/infobirds/spa/kensaku/onsenchi_detail.cfm?ID=207]{{ill|Japan Hot Spring Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Hot Spring Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60987269}}</ref> and there are also free foot baths as well as paid {{ill|communal baths|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/communal baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}}. Although the water quantity is abundant, its strongly acidic nature makes it unsuitable for recirculating equipment; therefore, all facilities use a "Flowing Bath" (flow-through) system, a point some establishments proudly promote with a "Source Flowing Bath Declaration."
In the area surrounding the hot spring town, there are also the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]]’s Kawayu Eco-Museum Center, the {{ill|Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|ja|大鵬相撲記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|1=Taihō Sumo Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487390}} (commemorating the achievements of the 48th Yokozuna [[:en:Taihō Kōki|Taihō Kōki]], who spent his childhood here), an indoor pool utilizing the hot spring heat, and the Kawayu Shrine, which gathers local devotional faith. === Kawayu Ekimae Onsen === A free foot bath is located in front of [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]]. Between the station and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]], there are two hot spring hotels/ryokan. The water quality here differs from that of Kawayu Onsen proper, being neutral and typically of the {{ill|sodium bicarbonate hot spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sodium bicarbonate hot spring|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} type. Some guides and introduction sites occasionally lump this facility together with Kawayu Onsen.
== History == Although the hot spring river has long been known to flow through the area, the origin of Kawayu as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} is uncertain. As for lodging, records indicate that a hot spring inn was established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), but it soon closed after becoming a gambling den for laborers working at Sulfur Mountain. Later, in [[:en:1904|1904]] (Meiji 37), a hot spring inn built in Russian style was established, which is considered the origin of the current Kawayu Onsen.
Until the Taishō period, only that one inn operated modestly, but during the Shōwa period, with the opening of automobile roads, the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]] opened in [[:en:1930|1930]] (Shōwa 5), and in [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) the {{ill|Akan National Park|ja|阿寒国立公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan National Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿寒摩周国立公園|1=Akan Mashu National Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415672}} was established, which spurred a rapid increase in therapeutic bathers.<ref>[http://www.iionsen.com/rekishi/donna.html What is Kawayu Onsen?] {{ill|Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja|川湯観光ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref>
During the 【[Second World War]】, tourism demand cooled, and soon after the war in [[:en:1948|1948]] (Shōwa 23), a major fire struck the area,<ref>[http://www.town.teshikaga.hokkaido.jp/teshikaga/gaiyou/gaiyou_data.html Timeline of the Town] [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]</ref> among other hardships. However, following the massive success of the film "{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Your Name|1=Kimi no Na wa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416227}}"—which featured the area around [[:en:Lake Kussharo|Kussharo Lake]] as a filming location—tourism surged once again, and the hot spring town rapidly developed.
Until the 1970s, winter road closures were common, but subsequent improvements in snow removal and road maintenance have established a system that attracts hot spring guests year-round. In its peak year, [[:en:1991|1991]] (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper=[[:en:Hokkaido Shimbun|Hokkaido Shimbun]]|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the [[:en:Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|Great East Japan Earthquake]] in [[:en:2011|2011]] (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the [[:en:2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake]] in [[:en:2018|2018]] (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of [[:en:2020|2020]] (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]]. * By Private Car: Via the [[:en:Dōtō Expressway|Dōtō Expressway]] from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along [[:en:Japan National Route 241|National Route 241]] and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]]. == References ==
{{Reflist}}

Longitude/Western Longitude) = E | Access = {{ill|JR Hokkaido|ja|JR北海道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Hokkaido|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北海道旅客鉄道|1=Hokkaido Railway Company|comment=changed title title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554939}} | Number of Lodging Facilities = | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | External Links = | Special Notes = }} [[File:Kawayu hot spring river.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Hot spring river flowing through the hot spring town]] [[File:川湯温泉駅足湯.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Foot bath at [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]]]] '''Kawayu Onsen''' (川湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]. Its name is an Ainu translation of “セセキ（hot）ペツ（river）.” A high-temperature hot spring river flows through the town.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}} {{ill|Acidic springs|ja|酸性泉|lt=Acidic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acidic springs|qq=}} {{ill|Alum springs|ja|含アルミニウム泉|lt=Alum spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alum springs|qq=}} * The water stings the eyes and wounds, and can even dissolve nails; because it corrodes precious metals, caution (such as not wearing wristwatches) is advised. * {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} precipitates and settles in the bath.
== Hot Spring Town == Centered on the source (湯元) of the hot spring river, the town is formed by over 20 establishments including [[:en:Hotel|hotels]], [[:en:Ryokan|ryokan]], souvenir shops, and restaurants. A hot spring river flows through the town, filling the area with steam and the scent of sulfur,<ref>[http://210.196.131.212/infobirds/spa/kensaku/onsenchi_detail.cfm?ID=207]{{ill|Japan Hot Spring Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Hot Spring Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60987269}}</ref> and there are also free foot baths as well as paid {{ill|communal baths|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/communal baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}}. Although the water quantity is abundant, its strongly acidic nature makes it unsuitable for recirculating equipment; therefore, all facilities use a "Flowing Bath" (flow-through) system, a point some establishments proudly promote with a "Source Flowing Bath Declaration."
In the area surrounding the hot spring town, there are also the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]]’s Kawayu Eco-Museum Center, the {{ill|Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|ja|大鵬相撲記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|1=Taihō Sumo Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487390}} (commemorating the achievements of the 48th Yokozuna [[:en:Taihō Kōki|Taihō Kōki]], who spent his childhood here), an indoor pool utilizing the hot spring heat, and the Kawayu Shrine, which gathers local devotional faith. === Kawayu Ekimae Onsen === A free foot bath is located in front of [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]]. Between the station and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]], there are two hot spring hotels/ryokan. The water quality here differs from that of Kawayu Onsen proper, being neutral and typically of the {{ill|sodium bicarbonate hot spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sodium bicarbonate hot spring|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} type. Some guides and introduction sites occasionally lump this facility together with Kawayu Onsen.
== History == Although the hot spring river has long been known to flow through the area, the origin of Kawayu as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} is uncertain. As for lodging, records indicate that a hot spring inn was established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), but it soon closed after becoming a gambling den for laborers working at Sulfur Mountain. Later, in [[:en:1904|1904]] (Meiji 37), a hot spring inn built in Russian style was established, which is considered the origin of the current Kawayu Onsen.
Until the Taishō period, only that one inn operated modestly, but during the Shōwa period, with the opening of automobile roads, the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]] opened in [[:en:1930|1930]] (Shōwa 5), and in [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) the {{ill|Akan National Park|ja|阿寒国立公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan National Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿寒摩周国立公園|1=Akan Mashu National Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415672}} was established, which spurred a rapid increase in therapeutic bathers.<ref>[http://www.iionsen.com/rekishi/donna.html What is Kawayu Onsen?] {{ill|Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja|川湯観光ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref>
During the 【[Second World War]】, tourism demand cooled, and soon after the war in [[:en:1948|1948]] (Shōwa 23), a major fire struck the area,<ref>[http://www.town.teshikaga.hokkaido.jp/teshikaga/gaiyou/gaiyou_data.html Timeline of the Town] [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]</ref> among other hardships. However, following the massive success of the film "{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Your Name|1=Kimi no Na wa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416227}}"—which featured the area around [[:en:Lake Kussharo|Kussharo Lake]] as a filming location—tourism surged once again, and the hot spring town rapidly developed.
Until the 1970s, winter road closures were common, but subsequent improvements in snow removal and road maintenance have established a system that attracts hot spring guests year-round. In its peak year, [[:en:1991|1991]] (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper=[[:en:Hokkaido Shimbun|Hokkaido Shimbun]]|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the [[:en:Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|Great East Japan Earthquake]] in [[:en:2011|2011]] (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the [[:en:2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake]] in [[:en:2018|2018]] (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of [[:en:2020|2020]] (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]]. * By Private Car: Via the [[:en:Dōtō Expressway|Dōtō Expressway]] from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along [[:en:Japan National Route 241|National Route 241]] and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]]. == References ==

43 | Latitude Minutes = 38 | Latitude Seconds = 16 | N (Northern Latitude/Southern Latitude) = N | Longitude Degrees = 144 | Longitude Minutes = 26 | Longitude Seconds = 14 | E (Eastern Longitude/Western Longitude) = E | Access = {{ill|JR Hokkaido|ja|JR北海道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Hokkaido|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北海道旅客鉄道|1=Hokkaido Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q498930}} [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]] – get off at [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]], then take the {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} to the "Kawayu Eigyōsho" (川湯営業所) stop for about 10 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}} ・ Acidic Alum Hot Spring | Spring Temperature = 35～65.5°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = {{ill|Strongly Acidic|ja|強酸性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Strongly Acidic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水素イオン指数|1=pH|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40936}} | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = {{ill|Hypotonic|ja|低張性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hypotonic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 泉質|1=senshitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554939}} | Number of Lodging Facilities = | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | External Links = | Special Notes = }} [[File:Kawayu hot spring river.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Hot spring river flowing through the hot spring town]] [[File:川湯温泉駅足湯.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Foot bath at [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]]]] '''Kawayu Onsen''' (川湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]. Its name is an Ainu translation of “セセキ（hot）ペツ（river）.” A high-temperature hot spring river flows through the town.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}} {{ill|Acidic springs|ja|酸性泉|lt=Acidic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acidic springs|qq=}} {{ill|Alum springs|ja|含アルミニウム泉|lt=Alum spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alum springs|qq=}} * The water stings the eyes and wounds, and can even dissolve nails; because it corrodes precious metals, caution (such as not wearing wristwatches) is advised. * {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} precipitates and settles in the bath.
== Hot Spring Town == Centered on the source (湯元) of the hot spring river, the town is formed by over 20 establishments including [[:en:Hotel|hotels]], [[:en:Ryokan|ryokan]], souvenir shops, and restaurants. A hot spring river flows through the town, filling the area with steam and the scent of sulfur,<ref>[http://210.196.131.212/infobirds/spa/kensaku/onsenchi_detail.cfm?ID=207]{{ill|Japan Hot Spring Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Hot Spring Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60987269}}</ref> and there are also free foot baths as well as paid {{ill|communal baths|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/communal baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}}. Although the water quantity is abundant, its strongly acidic nature makes it unsuitable for recirculating equipment; therefore, all facilities use a "Flowing Bath" (flow-through) system, a point some establishments proudly promote with a "Source Flowing Bath Declaration."
In the area surrounding the hot spring town, there are also the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]]’s Kawayu Eco-Museum Center, the {{ill|Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|ja|大鵬相撲記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|1=Taihō Sumo Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487390}} (commemorating the achievements of the 48th Yokozuna [[:en:Taihō Kōki|Taihō Kōki]], who spent his childhood here), an indoor pool utilizing the hot spring heat, and the Kawayu Shrine, which gathers local devotional faith. === Kawayu Ekimae Onsen === A free foot bath is located in front of [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]]. Between the station and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]], there are two hot spring hotels/ryokan. The water quality here differs from that of Kawayu Onsen proper, being neutral and typically of the {{ill|sodium bicarbonate hot spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sodium bicarbonate hot spring|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} type. Some guides and introduction sites occasionally lump this facility together with Kawayu Onsen.
== History == Although the hot spring river has long been known to flow through the area, the origin of Kawayu as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} is uncertain. As for lodging, records indicate that a hot spring inn was established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), but it soon closed after becoming a gambling den for laborers working at Sulfur Mountain. Later, in [[:en:1904|1904]] (Meiji 37), a hot spring inn built in Russian style was established, which is considered the origin of the current Kawayu Onsen.
Until the Taishō period, only that one inn operated modestly, but during the Shōwa period, with the opening of automobile roads, the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]] opened in [[:en:1930|1930]] (Shōwa 5), and in [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) the {{ill|Akan National Park|ja|阿寒国立公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan National Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿寒摩周国立公園|1=Akan Mashu National Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415672}} was established, which spurred a rapid increase in therapeutic bathers.<ref>[http://www.iionsen.com/rekishi/donna.html What is Kawayu Onsen?] {{ill|Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja|川湯観光ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref>
During the 【[Second World War]】, tourism demand cooled, and soon after the war in [[:en:1948|1948]] (Shōwa 23), a major fire struck the area,<ref>[http://www.town.teshikaga.hokkaido.jp/teshikaga/gaiyou/gaiyou_data.html Timeline of the Town] [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]</ref> among other hardships. However, following the massive success of the film "{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Your Name|1=Kimi no Na wa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416227}}"—which featured the area around [[:en:Lake Kussharo|Kussharo Lake]] as a filming location—tourism surged once again, and the hot spring town rapidly developed.
Until the 1970s, winter road closures were common, but subsequent improvements in snow removal and road maintenance have established a system that attracts hot spring guests year-round. In its peak year, [[:en:1991|1991]] (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper=[[:en:Hokkaido Shimbun|Hokkaido Shimbun]]|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the [[:en:Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|Great East Japan Earthquake]] in [[:en:2011|2011]] (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the [[:en:2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake]] in [[:en:2018|2018]] (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of [[:en:2020|2020]] (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]]. * By Private Car: Via the [[:en:Dōtō Expressway|Dōtō Expressway]] from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along [[:en:Japan National Route 241|National Route 241]] and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]]. == References ==

spring river.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Hot spring river flowing through the hot spring town]] [[File:川湯温泉駅足湯.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Foot bath at [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]]]] '''Kawayu Onsen''' (川湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]. Its name is an Ainu translation of “セセキ（hot）ペツ（river）.” A high-temperature hot spring river flows through the town.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}} {{ill|Acidic springs|ja|酸性泉|lt=Acidic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acidic springs|qq=}} {{ill|Alum springs|ja|含アルミニウム泉|lt=Alum spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alum springs|qq=}} * The water stings the eyes and wounds, and can even dissolve nails; because it corrodes precious metals, caution (such as not wearing wristwatches) is advised. * {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} precipitates and settles in the bath.
== Hot Spring Town == Centered on the source (湯元) of the hot spring river, the town is formed by over 20 establishments including [[:en:Hotel|hotels]], [[:en:Ryokan|ryokan]], souvenir shops, and restaurants. A hot spring river flows through the town, filling the area with steam and the scent of sulfur,<ref>[http://210.196.131.212/infobirds/spa/kensaku/onsenchi_detail.cfm?ID=207]{{ill|Japan Hot Spring Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Hot Spring Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60987269}}</ref> and there are also free foot baths as well as paid {{ill|communal baths|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/communal baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}}. Although the water quantity is abundant, its strongly acidic nature makes it unsuitable for recirculating equipment; therefore, all facilities use a "Flowing Bath" (flow-through) system, a point some establishments proudly promote with a "Source Flowing Bath Declaration."
In the area surrounding the hot spring town, there are also the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]]’s Kawayu Eco-Museum Center, the {{ill|Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|ja|大鵬相撲記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|1=Taihō Sumo Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487390}} (commemorating the achievements of the 48th Yokozuna [[:en:Taihō Kōki|Taihō Kōki]], who spent his childhood here), an indoor pool utilizing the hot spring heat, and the Kawayu Shrine, which gathers local devotional faith. === Kawayu Ekimae Onsen === A free foot bath is located in front of [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]]. Between the station and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]], there are two hot spring hotels/ryokan. The water quality here differs from that of Kawayu Onsen proper, being neutral and typically of the {{ill|sodium bicarbonate hot spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sodium bicarbonate hot spring|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} type. Some guides and introduction sites occasionally lump this facility together with Kawayu Onsen.
== History == Although the hot spring river has long been known to flow through the area, the origin of Kawayu as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} is uncertain. As for lodging, records indicate that a hot spring inn was established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), but it soon closed after becoming a gambling den for laborers working at Sulfur Mountain. Later, in [[:en:1904|1904]] (Meiji 37), a hot spring inn built in Russian style was established, which is considered the origin of the current Kawayu Onsen.
Until the Taishō period, only that one inn operated modestly, but during the Shōwa period, with the opening of automobile roads, the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]] opened in [[:en:1930|1930]] (Shōwa 5), and in [[:en:1934|1934]] (Shōwa 9) the {{ill|Akan National Park|ja|阿寒国立公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan National Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿寒摩周国立公園|1=Akan Mashu National Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415672}} was established, which spurred a rapid increase in therapeutic bathers.<ref>[http://www.iionsen.com/rekishi/donna.html What is Kawayu Onsen?] {{ill|Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja|川湯観光ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref>
During the 【[Second World War]】, tourism demand cooled, and soon after the war in [[:en:1948|1948]] (Shōwa 23), a major fire struck the area,<ref>[http://www.town.teshikaga.hokkaido.jp/teshikaga/gaiyou/gaiyou_data.html Timeline of the Town] [[:en:Teshikaga, Hokkaido|Teshikaga Town]]</ref> among other hardships. However, following the massive success of the film "{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Your Name|1=Kimi no Na wa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416227}}"—which featured the area around [[:en:Lake Kussharo|Kussharo Lake]] as a filming location—tourism surged once again, and the hot spring town rapidly developed.
Until the 1970s, winter road closures were common, but subsequent improvements in snow removal and road maintenance have established a system that attracts hot spring guests year-round. In its peak year, [[:en:1991|1991]] (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper=[[:en:Hokkaido Shimbun|Hokkaido Shimbun]]|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the [[:en:Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|Great East Japan Earthquake]] in [[:en:2011|2011]] (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the [[:en:2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake]] in [[:en:2018|2018]] (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of [[:en:2020|2020]] (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]]. * By Private Car: Via the [[:en:Dōtō Expressway|Dōtō Expressway]] from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along [[:en:Japan National Route 241|National Route 241]] and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]]. == References ==

In its peak year, [[:en:1991|1991]] (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper=[[:en:Hokkaido Shimbun|Hokkaido Shimbun]]|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the [[:en:Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|Great East Japan Earthquake]] in [[:en:2011|2011]] (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the [[:en:2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake]] in [[:en:2018|2018]] (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of [[:en:2020|2020]] (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as [[:en:Yunokawa Onsen|Yunokawa Onsen]] (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from [[:en:Kawayu-Onsen Station|Kawayu Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Senmō Main Line|Kushiro Main Line]]. * By Private Car: Via the [[:en:Dōtō Expressway|Dōtō Expressway]] from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along [[:en:Japan National Route 241|National Route 241]] and [[:en:Japan National Route 391|National Route 391]]. == References ==

== Related Topics == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]]

(北海道)]][[zh:川湯溫泉 (北海道)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)]][[ja:川湯温泉 (北海道)]][[zh:川湯溫泉 (北海道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11478935]] Wikidata name: Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11478935]] Wikidata name: Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Kawayu Onsen (川湯温泉) | image = Hot spring River in the Kawayu spa.jpg|thumb|200px|right | Comment = Free foot baths and the hot spring river flowing from them | Location = {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|WD=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido}} {{ill|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|en|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|WD=Q1186104|lt=Teshikaga Town}} | Latitude Degrees = 43 | Latitude Minutes = 38 | Latitude Seconds = 16 | N (Northern Latitude/Southern Latitude) = N | Longitude Degrees = 144 | Longitude Minutes = 26 | Longitude Seconds = 14 | E (Eastern Longitude/Western Longitude) = E | Access = {{ill|JR Hokkaido|ja|JR北海道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Hokkaido|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北海道旅客鉄道|1=Hokkaido Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q498930}} {{ill|Senmō Main Line|en|Senmō Main Line|WD=Q550432|lt=Kushiro Main Line}} – get off at {{ill|Kawayu-Onsen Station|en|Kawayu-Onsen Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}}, then take the {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} to the "Kawayu Eigyōsho" (川湯営業所) stop for about 10 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}} ・ Acidic Alum Hot Spring | Spring Temperature = 35～65.5°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = {{ill|Strongly Acidic|ja|強酸性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Strongly Acidic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水素イオン指数|1=pH|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40936}} | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = {{ill|Hypotonic|ja|低張性|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hypotonic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 泉質|1=senshitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554939}} | Number of Lodging Facilities = | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | External Links = | Special Notes = }} [[File:Kawayu hot spring river.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Hot spring river flowing through the hot spring town]] [[File:川湯温泉駅足湯.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Foot bath at {{ill|Kawayu-Onsen Station|en|Kawayu-Onsen Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}}]] '''Kawayu Onsen''' (川湯温泉) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|WD=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|en|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|WD=Q1186104|lt=Teshikaga Town}}. Its name is an Ainu translation of “セセキ（hot）ペツ（river）.” A high-temperature hot spring river flows through the town.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}} {{ill|Acidic springs|ja|酸性泉|lt=Acidic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acidic springs|qq=}} {{ill|Alum springs|ja|含アルミニウム泉|lt=Alum spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alum springs|qq=}} * The water stings the eyes and wounds, and can even dissolve nails; because it corrodes precious metals, caution (such as not wearing wristwatches) is advised. * {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} precipitates and settles in the bath.
== Hot Spring Town == Centered on the source (湯元) of the hot spring river, the town is formed by over 20 establishments including {{ill|Hotel|en|Hotel|WD=Q27686|lt=hotels}}, {{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=ryokan}}, souvenir shops, and restaurants. A hot spring river flows through the town, filling the area with steam and the scent of sulfur,<ref>[http://210.196.131.212/infobirds/spa/kensaku/onsenchi_detail.cfm?ID=207]{{ill|Japan Hot Spring Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Hot Spring Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60987269}}</ref> and there are also free foot baths as well as paid {{ill|communal baths|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/communal baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}}. Although the water quantity is abundant, its strongly acidic nature makes it unsuitable for recirculating equipment; therefore, all facilities use a "Flowing Bath" (flow-through) system, a point some establishments proudly promote with a "Source Flowing Bath Declaration."
In the area surrounding the hot spring town, there are also the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}}’s Kawayu Eco-Museum Center, the {{ill|Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|ja|大鵬相撲記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|1=Taihō Sumo Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487390}} (commemorating the achievements of the 48th Yokozuna {{ill|Taihō Kōki|en|Taihō Kōki|WD=Q319055|lt=Taihō Kōki}}, who spent his childhood here), an indoor pool utilizing the hot spring heat, and the Kawayu Shrine, which gathers local devotional faith. === Kawayu Ekimae Onsen === A free foot bath is located in front of {{ill|Kawayu-Onsen Station|en|Kawayu-Onsen Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}}. Between the station and {{ill|Japan National Route 391|en|Japan National Route 391|WD=Q6157421|lt=National Route 391}}, there are two hot spring hotels/ryokan. The water quality here differs from that of Kawayu Onsen proper, being neutral and typically of the {{ill|sodium bicarbonate hot spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sodium bicarbonate hot spring|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} type. Some guides and introduction sites occasionally lump this facility together with Kawayu Onsen.
== History == Although the hot spring river has long been known to flow through the area, the origin of Kawayu as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} is uncertain. As for lodging, records indicate that a hot spring inn was established in {{ill|1886|en|1886|WD=Q7822|lt=1886}} (Meiji 19), but it soon closed after becoming a gambling den for laborers working at Sulfur Mountain. Later, in {{ill|1904|en|1904|WD=Q2046|lt=1904}} (Meiji 37), a hot spring inn built in Russian style was established, which is considered the origin of the current Kawayu Onsen.
Until the Taishō period, only that one inn operated modestly, but during the Shōwa period, with the opening of automobile roads, the {{ill|Senmō Main Line|en|Senmō Main Line|WD=Q550432|lt=Kushiro Main Line}} opened in {{ill|1930|en|1930|WD=Q18787|lt=1930}} (Shōwa 5), and in {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} (Shōwa 9) the {{ill|Akan National Park|ja|阿寒国立公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan National Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿寒摩周国立公園|1=Akan Mashu National Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415672}} was established, which spurred a rapid increase in therapeutic bathers.<ref>[http://www.iionsen.com/rekishi/donna.html What is Kawayu Onsen?] {{ill|Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja|川湯観光ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref>
During the 【[Second World War]】, tourism demand cooled, and soon after the war in {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948}} (Shōwa 23), a major fire struck the area,<ref>[http://www.town.teshikaga.hokkaido.jp/teshikaga/gaiyou/gaiyou_data.html Timeline of the Town] {{ill|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|en|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|WD=Q1186104|lt=Teshikaga Town}}</ref> among other hardships. However, following the massive success of the film "{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Your Name|1=Kimi no Na wa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416227}}"—which featured the area around {{ill|Lake Kussharo|en|Lake Kussharo|WD=Q1329458|lt=Kussharo Lake}} as a filming location—tourism surged once again, and the hot spring town rapidly developed.
Until the 1970s, winter road closures were common, but subsequent improvements in snow removal and road maintenance have established a system that attracts hot spring guests year-round. In its peak year, {{ill|1991|en|1991|WD=Q2062|lt=1991}} (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper={{ill|Hokkaido Shimbun|en|Hokkaido Shimbun|WD=Q907792|lt=Hokkaido Shimbun}}|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the {{ill|Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q1136168|lt=Great East Japan Earthquake}} in {{ill|2011|en|2011|WD=Q1994|lt=2011}} (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the {{ill|2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|en|2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|WD=Q56483720|lt=Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake}} in {{ill|2018|en|2018|WD=Q25291|lt=2018}} (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of {{ill|2020|en|2020|WD=Q25337|lt=2020}} (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as {{ill|Yunokawa Onsen|en|Yunokawa Onsen|WD=Q8061358|lt=Yunokawa Onsen}} (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from {{ill|Kawayu-Onsen Station|en|Kawayu-Onsen Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}} on the {{ill|Senmō Main Line|en|Senmō Main Line|WD=Q550432|lt=Kushiro Main Line}}. * By Private Car: Via the {{ill|Dōtō Expressway|en|Dōtō Expressway|WD=Q867944|lt=Dōtō Expressway}} from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along {{ill|Japan National Route 241|en|Japan National Route 241|WD=Q6157013|lt=National Route 241}} and {{ill|Japan National Route 391|en|Japan National Route 391|WD=Q6157421|lt=National Route 391}}. == References ==

Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}} ・ Acidic Alum Hot Spring | Spring Temperature = 35～65.5°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = {{ill|Strongly Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | External Links = | Special Notes = }} [[File:Kawayu hot spring river.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Hot spring river flowing through the hot spring town]] [[File:川湯温泉駅足湯.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Foot bath at {{ill|Kawayu-Onsen Station|en|Kawayu-Onsen Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}}]] '''Kawayu Onsen''' (川湯温泉) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|WD=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|en|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|WD=Q1186104|lt=Teshikaga Town}}. Its name is an Ainu translation of “セセキ（hot）ペツ（river）.” A high-temperature hot spring river flows through the town.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}} {{ill|Acidic springs|ja|酸性泉|lt=Acidic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acidic springs|qq=}} {{ill|Alum springs|ja|含アルミニウム泉|lt=Alum spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alum springs|qq=}} * The water stings the eyes and wounds, and can even dissolve nails; because it corrodes precious metals, caution (such as not wearing wristwatches) is advised. * {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} precipitates and settles in the bath.
== Hot Spring Town == Centered on the source (湯元) of the hot spring river, the town is formed by over 20 establishments including {{ill|Hotel|en|Hotel|WD=Q27686|lt=hotels}}, {{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=ryokan}}, souvenir shops, and restaurants. A hot spring river flows through the town, filling the area with steam and the scent of sulfur,<ref>[http://210.196.131.212/infobirds/spa/kensaku/onsenchi_detail.cfm?ID=207]{{ill|Japan Hot Spring Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Hot Spring Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60987269}}</ref> and there are also free foot baths as well as paid {{ill|communal baths|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/communal baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}}. Although the water quantity is abundant, its strongly acidic nature makes it unsuitable for recirculating equipment; therefore, all facilities use a "Flowing Bath" (flow-through) system, a point some establishments proudly promote with a "Source Flowing Bath Declaration."
In the area surrounding the hot spring town, there are also the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}}’s Kawayu Eco-Museum Center, the {{ill|Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|ja|大鵬相撲記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|1=Taihō Sumo Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487390}} (commemorating the achievements of the 48th Yokozuna {{ill|Taihō Kōki|en|Taihō Kōki|WD=Q319055|lt=Taihō Kōki}}, who spent his childhood here), an indoor pool utilizing the hot spring heat, and the Kawayu Shrine, which gathers local devotional faith. === Kawayu Ekimae Onsen === A free foot bath is located in front of {{ill|Kawayu-Onsen Station|en|Kawayu-Onsen Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}}. Between the station and {{ill|Japan National Route 391|en|Japan National Route 391|WD=Q6157421|lt=National Route 391}}, there are two hot spring hotels/ryokan. The water quality here differs from that of Kawayu Onsen proper, being neutral and typically of the {{ill|sodium bicarbonate hot spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sodium bicarbonate hot spring|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} type. Some guides and introduction sites occasionally lump this facility together with Kawayu Onsen.
== History == Although the hot spring river has long been known to flow through the area, the origin of Kawayu as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} is uncertain. As for lodging, records indicate that a hot spring inn was established in {{ill|1886|en|1886|WD=Q7822|lt=1886}} (Meiji 19), but it soon closed after becoming a gambling den for laborers working at Sulfur Mountain. Later, in {{ill|1904|en|1904|WD=Q2046|lt=1904}} (Meiji 37), a hot spring inn built in Russian style was established, which is considered the origin of the current Kawayu Onsen.
Until the Taishō period, only that one inn operated modestly, but during the Shōwa period, with the opening of automobile roads, the {{ill|Senmō Main Line|en|Senmō Main Line|WD=Q550432|lt=Kushiro Main Line}} opened in {{ill|1930|en|1930|WD=Q18787|lt=1930}} (Shōwa 5), and in {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} (Shōwa 9) the {{ill|Akan National Park|ja|阿寒国立公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan National Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿寒摩周国立公園|1=Akan Mashu National Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415672}} was established, which spurred a rapid increase in therapeutic bathers.<ref>[http://www.iionsen.com/rekishi/donna.html What is Kawayu Onsen?] {{ill|Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja|川湯観光ホテル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen Tourism Hotel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}</ref>
During the 【[Second World War]】, tourism demand cooled, and soon after the war in {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948}} (Shōwa 23), a major fire struck the area,<ref>[http://www.town.teshikaga.hokkaido.jp/teshikaga/gaiyou/gaiyou_data.html Timeline of the Town] {{ill|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|en|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|WD=Q1186104|lt=Teshikaga Town}}</ref> among other hardships. However, following the massive success of the film "{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Your Name|1=Kimi no Na wa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416227}}"—which featured the area around {{ill|Lake Kussharo|en|Lake Kussharo|WD=Q1329458|lt=Kussharo Lake}} as a filming location—tourism surged once again, and the hot spring town rapidly developed.
Until the 1970s, winter road closures were common, but subsequent improvements in snow removal and road maintenance have established a system that attracts hot spring guests year-round. In its peak year, {{ill|1991|en|1991|WD=Q2062|lt=1991}} (Heisei 3), 734,000 overnight guests visited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title=Expecting Revival in Kawayu Onsen Town; "Bettei Nintō" Reopens on High-End Route / Budget Guest House Also Opens|date=2021-04-19|newspaper={{ill|Hokkaido Shimbun|en|Hokkaido Shimbun|WD=Q907792|lt=Hokkaido Shimbun}}|url=https://www.hokkaido-np.co.jp/article/534926|accessdate=2021-04-20|archiveurl=https://archive.ph/g67kj|archivedate=2021-04-20}}</ref>
However, thereafter the number of guests continued to decline. The self-restraint mood following the {{ill|Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q1136168|lt=Great East Japan Earthquake}} in {{ill|2011|en|2011|WD=Q1994|lt=2011}} (Heisei 23), as well as the effects of the {{ill|2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|en|2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi earthquake|WD=Q56483720|lt=Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake}} in {{ill|2018|en|2018|WD=Q25291|lt=2018}} (Heisei 30), also had significant impacts.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/|title=Kawayu Hotel Plaza Acquires Closed Hotels, Aiming to Reopen in January 2021|accessdate=2021-04-20|publisher={{ill|HotelBank|ja|ホテルバンク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HotelBank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|archivedate=2021-04-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20210419154425/https://hotelbank.jp/kawayu-suikazura/}}</ref> As of {{ill|2020|en|2020|WD=Q25337|lt=2020}} (Reiwa 2), due to ongoing poor business conditions, about 10 hotels and ryokan remain closed, with some having become abandoned ruins that pose a local landscape issue.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, the impact of the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染拡大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|1=COVID-19 pandemic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q81068910}} resulted in only 91,000 overnight guests in the 2020 fiscal year (Reiwa 2).
Regarding the origin of the name "Kawayu," when translating the Ainu term "セセキペツ" (meaning "hot river"), since a hot spring known as {{ill|Yunokawa Onsen|en|Yunokawa Onsen|WD=Q8061358|lt=Yunokawa Onsen}} (an Ainu translation of "ユベツ" meaning "hot water river") already existed in the vicinity of {{ill|Hakodate|ja|函館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate|1=Hakodate (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4494933}}, the order was reversed to form "Kawayu." == Access == * Rail: About 10 minutes by {{ill|Akan Bus|ja|阿寒バス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akan Bus|1=Akan Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657377}} from {{ill|Kawayu-Onsen Station|en|Kawayu-Onsen Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}} on the {{ill|Senmō Main Line|en|Senmō Main Line|WD=Q550432|lt=Kushiro Main Line}}. * By Private Car: Via the {{ill|Dōtō Expressway|en|Dōtō Expressway|WD=Q867944|lt=Dōtō Expressway}} from the {{ill|Ashiyori Interchange|ja|足寄インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyori Interchange|1=Ashoro interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636867}}, along {{ill|Japan National Route 241|en|Japan National Route 241|WD=Q6157013|lt=National Route 241}} and {{ill|Japan National Route 391|en|Japan National Route 391|WD=Q6157421|lt=National Route 391}}. == References ==

Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}}]] '''Kawayu Onsen''' (川湯温泉) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|WD=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|en|Teshikaga, Hokkaido|WD=Q1186104|lt=Teshikaga Town}}. Its name is an Ainu translation of “セセキ（hot）ペツ（river）.” A high-temperature hot spring river flows through the town.

Although the water quantity is abundant, its strongly acidic nature makes it unsuitable for recirculating equipment; therefore, all facilities use a "Flowing Bath" (flow-through) system, a point some establishments proudly promote with a "Source Flowing Bath Declaration."
In the area surrounding the hot spring town, there are also the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|WD=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment}}’s Kawayu Eco-Museum Center, the {{ill|Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|ja|大鵬相撲記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Sumo Memorial Hall|1=Taihō Sumo Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487390}} (commemorating the achievements of the 48th Yokozuna {{ill|Taihō Kōki|en|Taihō Kōki|WD=Q319055|lt=Taihō Kōki}}, who spent his childhood here), an indoor pool utilizing the hot spring heat, and the Kawayu Shrine, which gathers local devotional faith. === Kawayu Ekimae Onsen === A free foot bath is located in front of {{ill|Kawayu-Onsen Station|en|Kawayu-Onsen Station|WD=Q6379906|lt=Kawayu Onsen Station}}.
